long-title: Health information is too hard to read and has too much jargon

sections.long-title: What is the level being targeted?

content.choice-subtitle: Things that happen between individuals, like one-to-one support or care

content.long-title: Who should be involved?
content: People who find reading tricky
People who find counting tricky
People who use healthcare and other systems

content.long-title: What are the causes of the problem?
content: Low staff skills in health literacy
Lack of staff time to rewrite materials
Lack of resources to rewrite materials

content.long-title: What would help to solve the problem?
content: Using pictures, diagrams, videos and animations
Online and automated tools to help rewrite documents
Using Artificial intelligence (AI)

content.long-title: What actions are involved in doing something about this?
content: Rewriting materials so that they are easy to read
Giving staff training in health literacy
Making sure staff are trained often enough
Training clinical staff
Working with people receiving services
Working with graphic illustrators, film makers and animation makers

content.long-title: How will we deliver these actions?
content: Always explain codes and jargon used in materials
Authorised training including online and face-to-face options
Developing online materials
Work with people who can develop video and audio alternatives

content.long-title: What do we expect to come out of these actions?
content: Less anxiety for people reading and using information

content.long-title: How will we know things have changed?
content: Self-assessment questionnaires filled in by patients, members of the public and staff
Asking patients and the public what they think
Listening to what people think and feel about health information, and sharing what they say so it can help improve things

content.long-title: What will have changed in 1 year?
content: People will have better skills for health
Materials will be easier to use

content.long-title: What will have happened in 3 years?
content: Materials that are easier to use
Less stress and depression
More people completing treatment

content.choice-subtitle: Things that happen in organisations or communities, like teamwork or service coordination
content.long-title: What would help?
Who should be involved?
content: Staff at every level (within schools, healthcare, community services)
Health organisations (e.g., NHS trusts, clinics, charities)
Local authorities (e.g., public health teams, education departments)

content.long-title: What are the causes of the problem?
content: Staff in organisations don't know there is a problem
Organisations don't know there is a problem
Organisations don't have targets (e.g. specific measurable goals set to improve health literacy)
Health literacy is not currently part of the culture of organisations
Organisation systems are not fully accessible to staff (barriers within internal systems that prevent staff from easily accessing or using tools and resources to improve health literacy)

content.long-title: What would help to solve the problem?
content: Staff training
Sharing resources
Good organisation policies
Standardised information

content.long-title: What actions are involved in doing something about this?
content: Making the IT systems easier to use
Improving systems to include health literacy
Checking how complex materials are now (an audit)
Developing regional templates and systems

content.long-title: How will we deliver these actions?
content: System improvements
Applying updates across the system
Develop IT tools to help with simplifying health information

content.long-title: What do we expect to come out of these actions?
content: Health information that is easier to read and use
Health information in different formats
Health information available in other languages

content.long-title: How will we know things have changed?
content: Increased patient satisfaction
Lower readability levels of documents
Higher number and percentage of materials written in clear language
Higher number of languages in which health information is available

content.long-title: What will have changed in 1 year?
content: Staff will be happier and more engaged
Increased satisfaction with services
Systems will be easier to navigate
Better information for health

content.long-title: What will have happened in 3 years?
content: Patients happier with services
Better use of GP and hospital appointments
Fewer people missing appointments

content.choice-subtitle: Big system-wide things, like national policies or data systems
content.long-title: What would help?
Who should be involved?
content: People who run the systems (e.g. government departments, senior decision-makers, policy-makers)

content.long-title: What are the causes of the problem?
content: Different healthcare services and systems do not talk to each other

content.long-title: What would help to solve the problem?
content: Standardised systems
Better data systems to safely store information

content.long-title: What actions are involved in doing something about this?
content: Developing automated systems (e.g. broader infrastructure or technological solutions that can be scaled across systems to support health literacy improvements)

content.long-title: How will we deliver these actions?
content: Automated systems

content.long-title: What do we expect to come out of these actions?
content: Clear language used across systems

content.long-title: How will we know things have changed?
content: Quality of life scores will be higher

content.long-title: What will have changed in 1 year?
content: We will be developing action plans for how to improve things further
Systems will work better for people

content.long-title: What will have happened in 3 years?
content: The public will have improved levels of health literacy

sections.long-title: What are the current activities in this area?

content.long-title: Project one!
Project two!
